{
  "meta": {
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "title": "Hevy Planner",
    "subtitle": "Personalized Training Protocol",
    "description": "8-week joint restoration program with 12-month skills roadmap. Rebuilds shoulder stability, knee resilience, Achilles health, core strength, and thoracic mobility — then progresses to muscle-ups, tuck planche, pistol squats, and handstands.",
    "author": "AI Coach",
    "durationWeeks": 0,
    "tags": [
      "mobility",
      "joint-health",
      "rehabilitation",
      "calisthenics",
      "skills"
    ]
  },
  "exerciseTemplates": [
    {
      "id": "neck-cars",
      "title": "Neck CARs",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "neck",
      "notes": "Slow, controlled circles. Keep shoulders still. If you find a sticky spot, spend extra time there.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=neck+CARs+controlled+articular+rotations+tutorial",
      "searchTerms": "Neck CARs FRC tutorial"
    },
    {
      "id": "shoulder-cars",
      "title": "Shoulder CARs",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "shoulders",
      "notes": "CRITICAL for you. Make fist, lock elbow straight, trace the biggest circle possible. Go SLOW — 10 seconds per rotation minimum. Pain-free range only, expand it over weeks.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=shoulder+CARs+controlled+articular+rotations",
      "searchTerms": "Shoulder CARs FRC"
    },
    {
      "id": "hip-cars",
      "title": "Hip CARs (Standing)",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "glutes",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "hip_flexors"
      ],
      "notes": "Hold a wall for balance. Drive knee up, open out, extend back, return. Keep pelvis still — the rotation happens at the hip socket only.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=standing+hip+CARs+FRC+tutorial",
      "searchTerms": "Standing hip CARs FRC"
    },
    {
      "id": "ankle-cars",
      "title": "Ankle CARs",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "calves",
      "notes": "CRITICAL — especially right side (Achilles repair). Seated, lift foot off ground, make slow full circles. Note any clicking or restriction without forcing through it.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=ankle+CARs+controlled+articular+rotations",
      "searchTerms": "Ankle CARs FRC"
    },
    {
      "id": "spine-cars",
      "title": "Spine CARs (Segmental Cat-Cow)",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "lower_back",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "abdominals"
      ],
      "notes": "On all fours, roll through flexion and extension one vertebra at a time. Don't rush. This decompresses the spine after desk work.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=segmental+cat+cow+spine+CARs",
      "searchTerms": "Segmental cat-cow spine CARs"
    },
    {
      "id": "wrist-cars",
      "title": "Wrist CARs",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "forearms",
      "notes": "Make a fist, trace slow full circles at the wrist. You're doing planks, bear crawls, and bench pressing — your wrists take a beating. These also help you identify early warning signs of overuse before they become problems.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=wrist+CARs+controlled+articular+rotations",
      "searchTerms": "Wrist CARs FRC"
    },
    {
      "id": "tspine-rotation-quadruped",
      "title": "T-Spine Rotation (Quadruped)",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "upper_back",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "shoulders"
      ],
      "notes": "On all fours, place one hand behind your head. Rotate that elbow toward the ceiling, then down toward the opposite wrist. Follow your elbow with your eyes. This is the single most important addition for you — your desk-locked thoracic spine is the upstream bottleneck for your shoulders. If this doesn't rotate, your shoulder compensates during every press.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=quadruped+thoracic+rotation+mobility+drill",
      "searchTerms": "Quadruped thoracic rotation"
    },
    {
      "id": "dead-hangs",
      "title": "Dead Hangs",
      "type": "duration",
      "equipmentCategory": "pull_up_bar",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "lats",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "forearms",
        "shoulders"
      ],
      "notes": "Use your pull-up bar. Passive hang, let your shoulders stretch toward your ears. This decompresses the shoulder joint and builds grip. If 30s is easy, progress to active hangs (pull shoulders down while hanging).",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=dead+hang+shoulder+mobility+progression",
      "searchTerms": "Dead hang shoulder health"
    },
    {
      "id": "tspine-extension-foam-roller",
      "title": "T-Spine Extension Over Foam Roller",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "foam_roller",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "upper_back",
      "notes": "Place foam roller across your mid-back. Support your head with hands, elbows pointed forward. Extend back over the roller, then crunch forward — 10 reps. Then hold the extended position for 30 seconds. Move the roller up or down one vertebra and repeat. This directly unlocks the thoracic extension your shoulders need for overhead pressing and front rack position.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=thoracic+extension+foam+roller+mobility",
      "searchTerms": "T-spine extension foam roller",
      "tags": [
        "tspine-integration"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "open-book",
      "title": "Open Book (Thread the Needle)",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "upper_back",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "shoulders"
      ],
      "notes": "Lie on your side, knees stacked and bent at 90°. Top arm opens toward the ceiling and behind you while keeping knees together. Follow your hand with your eyes. The rotation should come from your mid-back, NOT your lower back — keeping your knees stacked prevents lumbar rotation. This is the movement pattern that lets you actually rotate during daily life without shoulder or low back compensation.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=open+book+thoracic+rotation+mobility+drill",
      "searchTerms": "Open book thoracic rotation",
      "tags": [
        "tspine-integration"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "scapular-pushups",
      "title": "Scapular Push-ups",
      "type": "bodyweight_reps",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "chest",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "shoulders"
      ],
      "notes": "Plank position, arms locked straight. Only your shoulder blades move — protract (spread apart) and retract (squeeze together). This trains serratus anterior, which is likely weak and causing your plank shoulder pain.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=scapular+push+ups+tutorial+serratus",
      "searchTerms": "Scapular push-ups serratus"
    },
    {
      "id": "banded-external-rotation",
      "title": "Banded External Rotation at 90°",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "resistance_band",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "shoulders",
      "notes": "Elbow at 90°, upper arm at shoulder height. Rotate forearm up against band resistance. Slow eccentric (3 sec lowering). This directly strengthens the rotator cuff in the position where it fails during pressing.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=banded+external+rotation+90+degrees+rotator+cuff",
      "searchTerms": "Banded external rotation 90 degrees"
    },
    {
      "id": "prone-ytw-raises",
      "title": "Prone Y-T-W Raises",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "dumbbell",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "shoulders",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "upper_back"
      ],
      "notes": "Lie face down on bench, light dumbbells (3-5 lb to start). Y = arms overhead in a V. T = arms straight out. W = elbows bent, squeeze shoulder blades. If 5 lb is too much, start bodyweight. These are humbling.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=prone+Y+T+W+raises+shoulder+rehab",
      "searchTerms": "Prone Y-T-W raises"
    },
    {
      "id": "wall-slides",
      "title": "Wall Slides",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "shoulders",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "upper_back"
      ],
      "notes": "Back and arms flat against wall. Slide arms up overhead keeping everything in contact with the wall. If your lower back arches off the wall, that's a mobility restriction — only go as high as you can maintain contact.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=wall+slides+shoulder+mobility+exercise",
      "searchTerms": "Wall slides shoulder mobility"
    },
    {
      "id": "shoulder-sleeper-stretch",
      "title": "Shoulder Sleeper Stretch with PAILs/RAILs",
      "type": "duration",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "shoulders",
      "notes": "Lie on your side, bottom arm at 90°. Use top hand to gently press into internal rotation. Hold 60s passive. Then PAILs: push bottom hand INTO the floor (10s, 20% effort building to 70%). Then RAILs: try to rotate hand UP without moving (10s same ramp). Re-stretch — you'll gain range.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=shoulder+PAILs+RAILs+sleeper+stretch+FRC",
      "searchTerms": "PAILs RAILs shoulder sleeper stretch"
    },
    {
      "id": "hollow-body-hold",
      "title": "Hollow Body Hold",
      "type": "duration",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "abdominals",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "hip_flexors"
      ],
      "notes": "Lie on your back, arms overhead, legs extended. Press lower back INTO the floor and lift shoulders and feet slightly off the ground. If your low back peels off the floor, bend your knees or bring arms to your sides to regress. This is the missing link behind your plank shoulder pain — when your core can't hold position, your shoulders compensate. Build this, and planks and pressing improve.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=hollow+body+hold+tutorial+progression",
      "searchTerms": "Hollow body hold progression",
      "tags": [
        "core-integration"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "ninety-ninety-hip-switches",
      "title": "90/90 Hip Switches",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "glutes",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "hip_flexors"
      ],
      "notes": "Sit with both legs at 90°. Rotate from one side to the other. Keep spine tall. If you can't get your knees to the floor, that's fine — work the range you have. This is your hip mobility barometer.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=90+90+hip+switches+mobility+tutorial",
      "searchTerms": "90/90 hip switches mobility"
    },
    {
      "id": "glute-bridge",
      "title": "Glute Bridge with 3-sec Hold",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "glutes",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "hamstrings"
      ],
      "notes": "Lie on back, knees bent, feet flat. Drive through heels, squeeze glutes HARD at the top, hold 3 seconds. Lower slowly. If you feel this in your hamstrings more than your glutes, bring your feet closer to your butt. This wakes up the glutes so they fire properly during squats, lunges, and walking. At 46 with desk work, your glutes are almost certainly underperforming — this fixes that.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=glute+bridge+hold+activation+tutorial",
      "searchTerms": "Glute bridge activation hold",
      "tags": [
        "glute-activation"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "banded-clamshells",
      "title": "Banded Clamshells",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "resistance_band",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "glutes",
      "notes": "Lie on your side, knees bent, band around knees. Open top knee against band resistance while keeping feet together. Slow and controlled — don't let the band snap your knee back down. This targets the gluteus medius, which stabilizes your pelvis and directly protects your knees during lateral movements. If your Cossack squats and side lunges hurt, weak glute med is likely a big contributor.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=banded+clamshell+glute+medius+activation",
      "searchTerms": "Banded clamshell glute medius",
      "tags": [
        "glute-activation"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "tibialis-raises",
      "title": "Tibialis Anterior Raises",
      "type": "bodyweight_reps",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "calves",
      "notes": "Stand with back against wall, feet ~12 inches out. Pull toes up toward shins, lower slowly. This strengthens the front of your shin and protects the knee. The Knees Over Toes Guy made this famous for good reason.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=tibialis+raises+knees+over+toes+tutorial",
      "searchTerms": "Tibialis raises Knees Over Toes"
    },
    {
      "id": "banded-tke",
      "title": "Banded Terminal Knee Extensions",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "resistance_band",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "quadriceps",
      "notes": "Attach band behind your knee to a low anchor. Stand facing away, slight bend. Squeeze to full lockout against band resistance. This isolates the VMO — the inner quad muscle that stabilizes the kneecap.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=banded+terminal+knee+extension+VMO",
      "searchTerms": "Banded TKE VMO strengthening"
    },
    {
      "id": "atg-split-squat",
      "title": "ATG Split Squat (Regressed)",
      "type": "bodyweight_reps",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "quadriceps",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "glutes",
        "calves"
      ],
      "notes": "Start with hands on bench/chair for support. Back knee tracks all the way down and forward. ONLY go as deep as you can control without pain. Weeks 1-2: use support. Weeks 3-4: reduce support. This is the single best knee bulletproofing exercise.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=ATG+split+squat+regression+beginner+knees+over+toes",
      "searchTerms": "ATG split squat beginner regression",
      "progressionByPhase": {
        "Foundation": {
          "sets": "3×8 each leg",
          "notes": "Use bench support, partial depth only."
        },
        "Build": {
          "sets": "3×8 each leg",
          "notes": "Reduce bench support, increase depth."
        },
        "Load": {
          "sets": "3×8 each leg",
          "notes": "Light dumbbells, no support."
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "cossack-squat",
      "title": "Cossack Squat (Partial ROM)",
      "type": "bodyweight_reps",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "quadriceps",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "glutes",
        "hamstrings"
      ],
      "notes": "Bodyweight only. Start with a small range — you do NOT need to go ass-to-grass. Hold a doorframe or rack for balance. This directly trains the lateral movement pattern that's giving your knees trouble. Depth will come with consistency.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=cossack+squat+beginner+progression+mobility",
      "searchTerms": "Cossack squat progression beginner",
      "progressionByPhase": {
        "Foundation": {
          "sets": "3×6 each side",
          "notes": "Hold doorframe, go only as deep as comfortable."
        },
        "Build": {
          "sets": "3×6 each side",
          "notes": "Less support, aim for parallel thigh depth."
        },
        "Load": {
          "sets": "3×6 each side",
          "notes": "Goblet Cossack Squats with light dumbbell."
        }
      }

      "description": "Your shoulders, knees, and ankles all depend on a stable trunk. When the core can't hold position, your joints compensate — that's a big part of why you keep getting injured.           "sets": "3×30 sec",
          "notes": "Arms overhead, legs extended, low back pressed into floor, shoulders and feet lifted. If you can hold 30s at the hardest version, you can progress to hollow body rocks."
        },
        {
          "exerciseTemplateId": "copenhagen-plank",
          "sets": "3×20-30 sec each side",
          "notes": "Side plank with top leg on bench, bottom leg free. Trains lateral core and adductors simultaneously. Regression: bend bottom knee to floor, or use a shorter bench so you're supporting from the knee instead of the foot. Progression: add hip dips (lower and lift hips while holding). This exercise alone can fix a lot of your lateral movement issues."

      "id": "foundation",

      "details": [

        "Cossack Squat: hold doorframe, go only as deep as comfortable",
        "Eccentric calf raises: both-leg lower if single-leg is too intense on the right",
        "Dead hangs: aim for 15-20s, build to 30s",
        "Core: Dead bugs with bent knees, hollow body with knees bent and arms at sides, Copenhagen plank from knees (10-15s)"

        "Y-T-W raises: increase dumbbell weight by 2-3 lb",
        "Begin testing overhead press and front squat with improved positions",

      ]

  ],
  "roadmap": [
    {
      "id": "phase1",
      "name": "Phase 1: Joint Restoration",
      "weeks": "Weeks 1-8",
      "status": "current",
      "summary": "This is the program you're in now. Rebuild shoulder stability, knee resilience, Achilles health, core strength, and thoracic mobility. No skill work yet — your joints aren't ready.",
      "keyFocus": "The temptation will be to start skill work early. Don't. Every week of foundation you invest here saves you months of plateau and re-injury later. If you fail any gate test at week 8, extend Phase 1 by 2-week increments until you pass.",
      "gateTests": [
        "pain-free-planks",
        "strict-pullups-8",
        "clean-dips-15",
        "cossack-squat-full",
        "single-leg-balance-30",
        "wall-dorsiflexion-4in",
        "hollow-body-30s",
        "overhead-wall-test"
      ],
      "color": "rgb(96, 165, 224)",
      "sortOrder": 1
    },
    {
      "id": "phase2",
      "name": "Phase 2: Strength Prerequisites",
      "weeks": "Weeks 9-20 (12 weeks)",
      "status": "future",
      "summary": "Build the raw strength and positions your skills require. Shoulder strength, straight-arm conditioning, single-leg loading, and wrist preparation. Daily CARs and core work continue as maintenance.",
      "keyFocus": "This phase is dominated by pull-up volume, dip volume, and straight-arm conditioning. If you're doing BODi, use it for lower body days and replace upper body BODi with the skill-prep work. The false grip and straight-arm work will feel completely foreign — your tendons need time to adapt.",
      "gateTests": [
        "strict-pullups-12",
        "parallel-dips-15",
        "false-grip-20s",
        "ring-support-30s",
        "pistol-to-box",
        "wall-handstand-45s",
        "planche-lean-10s",
        "scapular-pullups-3x8"
      ],
      "color": "rgb(224, 134, 96)",
      "sortOrder": 2
    },
    {
      "id": "phase3",
      "name": "Phase 3: Skill Acquisition",
      "weeks": "Weeks 21-36 (16 weeks)",
      "status": "future",
      "summary": "Active skill practice begins. Muscle-up transition training, planche lean progressions, pistol squat depth development, and handstand balance work. This is as much neurological as physical.",
      "keyFocus": "Skill days replace some strength days. You're now practicing 3-4 skills per week alongside maintenance mobility and strength. The muscle-up will likely click before the planche — the transition is a technique breakthrough moment. Film every attempt.",
      "gateTests": [
        "muscle-ups-1-3",
        "tuck-planche-5s",
        "pistol-squat-full",
        "freestanding-handstand-20s",
        "negative-muscle-up-3s",

      ],
      "color": "rgb(96, 200, 140)",

    },
    {
      "id": "phase4",
      "name": "Phase 4: Refinement & Beyond",
      "weeks": "Weeks 37-52+ (ongoing)",
      "status": "future",
      "summary": "Polish skill quality, add reps, and begin harder progressions. Multiple clean muscle-ups, advanced planche variations, loaded pistol squats, freestanding handstand confidence.",
      "keyFocus": "This becomes your ongoing training life. Rotate focus between skills based on interest and progress. The planche journey continues well beyond a year — enjoy the process. The muscle-up becomes a warm-up exercise you can rep out.",
      "gateTests": [
        "consecutive-muscle-ups-3-5",
        "tuck-planche-10s",
        "freestanding-handstand-15s",
        "pistol-squat-no-counterbalance",
        "advanced-tuck-planche-3-5s"
      ],
      "color": "rgb(180, 140, 224)",
      "sortOrder": 4
    }
  ],
  "skills": [
    {
      "id": "muscle-up",
      "name": "Muscle Up",
      "priority": 1,
      "icon": "★",
      "color": "rgb(224, 134, 96)",
      "currentState": "3-5 pull-ups (previously 7-10 before shoulder issues). No muscle-up experience. Shoulder pain during planks and pressing.",
      "requirements": "12+ strict pull-ups, 15+ dips, false grip strength, explosive pulling power, deep shoulder extension under load, and the transition technique.",
      "gapAnalysis": "Your pulling strength base exists but is suppressed by shoulder instability. Once shoulders stabilize (Phase 1), pull-up numbers will rebound quickly since the muscle memory is there. The false grip and transition are completely new skills. The deep shoulder extension required at the transition point is the riskiest part given your shoulder history — this is why we must not rush.",
      "timeline": "First muscle-up: 6-9 months (weeks 24-36). Confident reps: 9-12 months.",
      "milestones": [
        {
          "name": "8-10 strict pull-ups",
          "description": "Rebuild pulling base after shoulder stabilization",
          "targetWeek": 8
        },
        {
          "name": "12-15 strict pull-ups",
          "description": "Strength prerequisite for explosive pulling",
          "targetWeek": 20
        },
        {
          "name": "False grip hang 20+ sec",
          "description": "Wrist and forearm conditioning for transition",
          "targetWeek": 16
        },
        {
          "name": "High pull-ups to sternum",
          "description": "Explosive pulling height needed for transition",
          "targetWeek": 20
        },
        {
          "name": "Negative muscle-up (5 sec descent)",
          "description": "Eccentric control through the transition",
          "targetWeek": 24
        },
        {
          "name": "First muscle-up",
          "description": "Bar or rings, any style",
          "targetWeek": 30
        },
        {
          "name": "3-5 consecutive muscle-ups",
          "description": "Confident, repeatable reps",
          "targetWeek": 44
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "tuck-planche",
      "name": "Tuck Planche",
      "priority": 2,
      "icon": "◆",
      "color": "rgb(180, 140, 224)",
      "currentState": "No planche training history. Shoulder pain limits plank position. Scapular stabilizer weakness identified.",
      "requirements": "Strong scapular protraction, straight-arm pressing strength, progressive lean tolerance, conditioned wrists. The tuck planche is the most achievable planche variation and still looks impressive — hips level with shoulders, knees tucked to chest, body floating on hands.",
      "gapAnalysis": "The tuck planche requires straight-arm pressing with protracted scapulae that standard lifting doesn't build. Your shoulders need to be fully stable before you start leaning. The good news: tuck planche is a realistic 9-15 month goal, well within the same timeline as your muscle-up. Advanced tuck (legs partially extended) is the stretch goal beyond that.",
      "timeline": "Planche lean: 4-6 months. Frog stand: 3-4 months. Tuck planche (2-3 sec): 9-12 months. Tuck planche (5-10 sec): 12-15 months. Advanced tuck planche: 15-24 months (stretch goal).",
      "milestones": [
        {
          "name": "Pain-free plank 60+ sec",
          "description": "Baseline pressing endurance",
          "targetWeek": 8
        },
        {
          "name": "Frog stand hold",
          "description": "Balance and wrist conditioning",
          "targetWeek": 14
        },
        {
          "name": "Planche lean 20+ degrees",
          "description": "Progressive forward lean tolerance",
          "targetWeek": 20
        },
        {
          "name": "Tuck planche 2-3 sec",
          "description": "First hold with hips level",
          "targetWeek": 36
        },
        {
          "name": "Tuck planche 5-10 sec",
          "description": "Solid, repeatable hold",
          "targetWeek": 48
        },
        {
          "name": "Advanced tuck planche 3-5 sec",

        }
      ]

    {
      "id": "pistol-squat",
      "name": "Pistol Squat",
      "priority": 3,
      "icon": "●",
      "color": "rgb(96, 200, 140)",
      "currentState": "Limited depth on pistol squats. Lateral lunge knee pain. Surgically repaired right Achilles limiting dorsiflexion. Tight hips.",
      "requirements": "Full ankle dorsiflexion (especially right side), strong VMO and quad, hip flexor strength to hold the non-working leg extended, single-leg balance, and hip mobility to sit deep without butt wink.",
      "gapAnalysis": "Your three limiters are exactly what Phase 1 targets: ankle dorsiflexion, knee stability, and hip mobility. The specific gap is single-leg strength and hip flexor endurance. Your right side will always be harder due to the Achilles — accept asymmetry and work both sides to their respective maximums.",
      "timeline": "Pistol to box (16 inch): 8-12 weeks. Pistol to box (8 inch): 16-20 weeks. Full-depth pistol (both legs): 5-8 months. Right leg may lag 2-4 weeks behind left.",
      "milestones": [

      "gapAnalysis": "Your t-spine restriction and shoulder instability are the current blockers. Once those resolve (Phase 1-2), the handstand becomes primarily a balance skill. At 46, balance adaptation is slower but absolutely happens — expect more time in wall-supported positions. Your core strength from Session D will transfer directly.",

  "benchmarks": [
    {
      "id": "wall-dorsiflexion",
      "name": "Wall Dorsiflexion Test",
      "howTo": "Face wall, foot back from wall. Drive knee forward keeping heel down. Measure max distance (inches) where knee touches wall and heel stays down.",
      "target": "Left: 4-5 inches. Right (Achilles): will start lower — track improvement, don't compare sides.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 2 weeks",
      "unit": "inches"
    },
    {
      "id": "single-leg-balance-test",
      "name": "Single-Leg Balance (Eyes Closed)",
      "howTo": "Stand on one foot, close eyes, time how long before you touch down or open eyes.",
      "target": "30 seconds each side. Right ankle will lag — that's expected.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 2 weeks",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "hollow-body-hold-test",
      "name": "Hollow Body Hold Time",
      "howTo": "Full position (arms overhead, legs straight). Time until low back lifts off floor.",
      "target": "Week 1: 10-15s is normal. Week 8 goal: 30-45s.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 3 weeks",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "overhead-reach-test",
      "name": "Overhead Reach (Wall Test)",
      "howTo": "Stand with back, butt, and head against wall. Raise arms overhead trying to touch thumbs to wall without arching your low back off the wall.",
      "target": "Thumbs touch wall with back flat = good t-spine and shoulder mobility. If back arches, that's your t-spine restriction.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 3 weeks"
    },
    {
      "id": "deep-squat-test",
      "name": "Deep Squat Assessment",
      "howTo": "Feet shoulder width, squat as deep as possible without any support. Note: heels lifting, excessive forward lean, knees caving, inability to keep chest up.",
      "target": "Hips below parallel, heels down, chest up, no pain. Use a phone to film yourself — you'll miss compensations otherwise.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, film it. Re-film every 4 weeks."
    },
    {
      "id": "ninety-ninety-hip-test",
      "name": "90/90 Hip Test",
      "howTo": "Sit in 90/90 position (both legs at 90°). Can you sit tall with both knees touching or near the floor?",
      "target": "Both knees within 3-4 inches of floor, spine upright without hands for support.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 3 weeks"
    },
    {
      "id": "cross-body-shoulder-reach",
      "name": "Cross-Body Shoulder Reach",
      "howTo": "Reach right hand behind your back from below, left hand from above. How close can your fingertips get? Repeat on the other side.",
      "target": "Fingers touching or overlapping. Note the difference between sides — your tighter side is the priority.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 4 weeks"
    },
    {
      "id": "pain-free-planks",
      "name": "Pain-Free Planks",
      "howTo": "Hold a standard plank position. Note any shoulder pain during or after.",
      "target": "45+ seconds with zero shoulder pain."
    },
    {
      "id": "strict-pullups-8",
      "name": "Strict Pull-ups (Phase 1 Gate)",
      "howTo": "Dead hang start, pull chin over bar, full lockout between reps. No kipping.",
      "target": "8-10 strict pull-ups."
    },
    {
      "id": "clean-dips-15",
      "name": "Clean Dips (Phase 1 Gate)",
      "howTo": "Parallel bars or bench dips, full range of motion, controlled tempo.",
      "target": "15+ clean dips."
    },
    {
      "id": "cossack-squat-full",
      "name": "Cossack Squat Full Depth",
      "howTo": "Full-depth bodyweight Cossack squat each side, no external support.",
      "target": "Full depth both sides, no support, no pain."
    },
    {
      "id": "single-leg-balance-30",
      "name": "Single-Leg Balance 30s (Gate)",
      "howTo": "Stand on one foot, close eyes. Time until loss of balance.",
      "target": "30 seconds each side, eyes closed.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "wall-dorsiflexion-4in",
      "name": "Wall Dorsiflexion 4+ Inches",
      "howTo": "Face wall, drive knee forward. Measure distance from wall where heel stays down.",
      "target": "4+ inches each side.",
      "unit": "inches"
    },
    {
      "id": "hollow-body-30s",
      "name": "Hollow Body Hold 30s (Gate)",
      "howTo": "Arms overhead, legs straight. Hold until low back lifts off floor.",
      "target": "30+ seconds, arms overhead.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "overhead-wall-test",
      "name": "Overhead Wall Test (Gate)",
      "howTo": "Back against wall, raise arms overhead keeping back flat.",
      "target": "Thumbs touch wall, back stays flat."
    },
    {
      "id": "strict-pullups-12",
      "name": "Strict Pull-ups (Phase 2 Gate)",
      "howTo": "Dead hang start, strict form, full range.",
      "target": "12-15 strict pull-ups."
    },
    {
      "id": "parallel-dips-15",
      "name": "Parallel Bar Dips (Phase 2 Gate)",
      "howTo": "Parallel bars, full depth, controlled tempo.",
      "target": "15-20 parallel bar dips."
    },
    {
      "id": "false-grip-20s",
      "name": "False Grip Hang",
      "howTo": "Hang from bar or rings with false grip (wrist over bar). Time the hold.",
      "target": "20+ seconds.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "ring-support-30s",
      "name": "Ring Support Hold",
      "howTo": "Support position on rings, shoulders externally rotated (rings turned out).",
      "target": "30-second hold with external rotation.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "pistol-to-box",
      "name": "Pistol Squat to Box",
      "howTo": "Stand on one leg, lower to bench/box (12-16 inch height), stand back up.",
      "target": "Controlled descent and ascent each leg."
    },
    {
      "id": "wall-handstand-45s",
      "name": "Wall Handstand Hold",
      "howTo": "Stomach facing wall, nose almost touching. Hold inverted position.",
      "target": "45-second hold.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "planche-lean-10s",
      "name": "Planche Lean",
      "howTo": "Hands on floor, lean forward shifting weight over hands at 20+ degree angle.",
      "target": "10-second hold at 20+ degrees.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "scapular-pullups-3x8",
      "name": "Scapular Pull-ups",
      "howTo": "From dead hang, depress shoulders (pull shoulder blades down), return to passive hang.",
      "target": "3×8 full range on bar."
    },
    {
      "id": "muscle-ups-1-3",
      "name": "Muscle-ups (Phase 3 Gate)",
      "howTo": "From dead hang, pull up and transition over the bar or rings to support position.",
      "target": "1-3 muscle-ups (bar or rings)."
    },
    {
      "id": "tuck-planche-5s",
      "name": "Tuck Planche Hold",
      "howTo": "Hands on floor or parallettes, tuck knees to chest, lift hips level with shoulders.",
      "target": "5-second hold on floor or parallettes."
    },
    {
      "id": "pistol-squat-full",
      "name": "Full Pistol Squat",
      "howTo": "Single-leg squat to full depth. Counterbalance with a light weight is acceptable.",
      "target": "Full-depth each leg, controlled tempo."
    },
    {
      "id": "freestanding-handstand-20s",
      "name": "Freestanding Handstand",
      "howTo": "Kick up to handstand in open space. Hold without wall support.",
      "target": "20-second hold OR 45-second wall handstand with controlled entry/exit.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "negative-muscle-up-3s",
      "name": "Negative Muscle-up",
      "howTo": "Start in support position on bar, lower slowly through transition to hang.",
      "target": "3-second controlled descent.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "tuck-planche-level",
      "name": "Tuck Planche (Hips Level)",
      "howTo": "Tuck planche with hips at shoulder height, back flat.",
      "target": "Hips level with shoulders, held position."
    },
    {
      "id": "consecutive-muscle-ups-3-5",
      "name": "Consecutive Muscle-ups",
      "howTo": "Multiple muscle-ups in a row without dismounting.",
      "target": "3-5 consecutive muscle-ups."
    },
    {
      "id": "tuck-planche-10s",
      "name": "Tuck Planche 10s Hold",
      "howTo": "Tuck planche position held for duration.",
      "target": "10+ seconds.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "freestanding-handstand-15s",
      "name": "Freestanding Handstand 15s",
      "howTo": "Freestanding handstand held for duration.",
      "target": "15+ seconds.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "pistol-squat-no-counterbalance",
      "name": "Full Pistol Squat (No Counterbalance)",
      "howTo": "Full-depth single-leg squat, arms free (no weight held for balance).",
      "target": "Full pistol each leg, no counterbalance."
    },
    {
      "id": "advanced-tuck-planche-3-5s",
      "name": "Advanced Tuck Planche",
      "howTo": "Tuck planche with legs partially extended from tuck position.",
      "target": "3-5 seconds (stretch goal).",
      "unit": "seconds"
    }
  ],
  "foundations": [
    {
      "id": "breathing-bracing",
      "title": "Breathing & Bracing Protocol",
      "description": "Proper breathing and bracing is the foundation of all loaded movement. Master this before adding intensity to any exercise.",
      "steps": [
        {
          "step": 1,
          "name": "Find Your Diaphragm",
          "instructions": "Lie on your back, knees bent. Place one hand on your chest, one on your belly. Breathe in through your nose — ONLY the belly hand should rise. Practice until you can take 10 breaths where only your belly moves.",
          "videoSearch": "diaphragmatic breathing tutorial lying"
        },
        {
          "step": 2,
          "name": "360 Breathing",
          "instructions": "Place hands on your sides. When you inhale, your belly, sides, AND lower back should all expand outward like inflating a cylinder. Practice 10 breaths in the 90/90 position.",
          "videoSearch": "360 degree breathing core stability drill"
        },
        {
          "step": 3,
          "name": "Bracing Under Load",
          "instructions": "Take a 360 breath in. Without exhaling, tighten your midsection as if someone is about to punch you in the gut. Inhale at the easy part, brace, execute the hard part, exhale at the top.",
          "videoSearch": "bracing core squat university breathing"
        }
      ],
      "practice": "Do 10 diaphragmatic breaths before daily CARs for the first 2 weeks.",
      "activeDuringWeeks": {
        "start": 1,
        "end": 4
      }
    }
  ],

lines: "description": "Your shoulders, knees, and ankles all depend on a stable trunk. When the core can't hold position, your joints compensate — that's a big part of why you keep getting injured. This session trains anti-extension, anti-rotation, and anti-lateral-flexion — the three pillars of functional core stability. These aren't crunches; they teach your core to resist unwanted movement so your limbs can move freely. Exercises marked with ◆ in other sessions are drawn from here.",

{
  "meta": {
    "version": "1.0.0",

      "id": "core-stability",
      "title": "Session D: Core Stability",
      "subtitle": "15-20 min — Do 1-2x per week",
      "description": "Your shoulders, knees, and ankles all depend on a stable trunk. When the core can't hold position, your joints compensate — that's a big part of why you keep getting injured. This session trains anti-extension, anti-rotation, and anti-lateral-flexion — the three pillars of functional core stability. These aren't crunches; they teach your core to resist unwanted movement so your limbs can move freely. Exercises marked with ◆ in other sessions are drawn from here.",
      "folderGroup": "Main Sessions",
      "sortOrder": 5,
      "color": "rgb(224, 120, 150)",
      "exercises": [
        {
          "exerciseTemplateId": "dead-bugs",
          "sets": "3×10 each side",
          "notes": "The king of core exercises for your situation. Lie on back, arms up, knees at 90°. Press lower back into floor — this is the cue that matters. Extend opposite arm and leg while maintaining ZERO low back movement. Slow and controlled — 3 seconds out, 3 seconds back. If your back arches, shorten the range. Progress by holding a light dumbbell overhead or adding a band around your feet."
        },
        {
          "exerciseTemplateId": "pallof-press",
          "sets": "3×10 each side"
        },

          "exerciseTemplateId": "bird-dogs",
          "sets": "3×8 each side"
        },
        {
          "exerciseTemplateId": "hollow-body-hold",
          "sets": "3×30 sec",
          "notes": "Arms overhead, legs extended, low back pressed into floor, shoulders and feet lifted. Scale it: bent knees with arms at sides (easiest) → straight legs with arms at sides → straight legs with arms overhead (hardest). If you can hold 30s at the hardest version, you can progress to hollow body rocks."
        },
        {
          "exerciseTemplateId": "copenhagen-plank",
          "sets": "3×20-30 sec each side",
          "notes": "Side plank with top leg on bench, bottom leg free. Trains lateral core and adductors simultaneously. Regression: bend bottom knee to floor, or use a shorter bench so you're supporting from the knee instead of the foot. Progression: add hip dips (lower and lift hips while holding). This exercise alone can fix a lot of your lateral movement issues."
        },

          "sets": "3×30 sec each side"
        }

        "Cossack Squat: hold doorframe, go only as deep as comfortable",
        "Eccentric calf raises: both-leg lower if single-leg is too intense on the right",
        "Dead hangs: aim for 15-20s, build to 30s",
        "Core: Dead bugs with bent knees, hollow body with knees bent and arms at sides, Copenhagen plank from knees (10-15s)"
      ]
    },

      "id": "build",

      "details": [

      {
        "name": "Dead Bug",
        "index": 0,
        "exerciseTemplateId": "D8911FC4",
        "notes": "The king of core exercises for your situation. Lie on back, arms up, knees at 90°. Press lower back into floor — this is the cue that matters. Extend opposite arm and leg while maintaining ZERO low back movement. Slow and controlled — 3 seconds out, 3 seconds back. If your back arches, shorten the range. Progress by holding a light dumbbell overhead or adding a band around your feet.",

content.text:
Good — 6 routines in 1 "Hevy Planner" folder, no duplicates. These are the ones from the last setup. When we run setup again, the reconciliation will match them by title and update them in place (moving them to the correct folders).

Now let me start the server.

input.command: npx wrangler d1 migrations apply hevy-planner --local && npx wrangler dev --local --persist-to .wrangler/state
input.description: Apply migrations and start dev server

content.content: Command running in background with ID: bdceeo1xo. Output is being written to: /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-ryanr-Code-hevy-planner/efca647b-adeb-4816-9a5e-8d564bffcace/tasks/bdceeo1xo.output

input.description: Wait for server and check it's up

content.text:
Server is up at http://localhost:8787. DB is fresh — go ahead and run through setup. The reconciliation should:

1. Create 3 new folders: **Daily**, **Main Sessions**, **Recovery**
2. Match existing routines by title against the old "Hevy Planner" folder (since the new folders don't exist yet, those won't match) — so it will create new routines in the correct folders
3. Today page should show "Open in Hevy" deep links

After setup, we can clean up the old "Hevy Planner" folder routines.

      {
        "name": "Dead Bug",
        "index": 0,
        "exerciseTemplateId": "D8911FC4",
        "notes": "The king of core exercises for your situation. Lie on back, arms up, knees at 90°. Press lower back into floor — this is the cue that matters. Extend opposite arm and leg while maintaining ZERO low back movement. Slow and controlled — 3 seconds out, 3 seconds back. If your back arches, shorten the range. Progress by holding a light dumbbell overhead or adding a band around your feet.",

    "exercises": [
      {
        "name": "Dead Bug",
        "index": 0,
        "exerciseTemplateId": "D8911FC4",
        "notes": "The king of core exercises for your situation. Lie on back, arms up, knees at 90°. Press lower back into floor — this is the cue that matters. Extend opposite arm and leg while maintaining ZERO low back movement. Slow and controlled — 3 seconds out, 3 seconds back. If your back arches, shorten the range. Progress by holding a light dumbbell overhead or adding a band around your feet.",

        "sets": [

      {
        "name": "Hollow Body Hold",
        "index": 3,
        "exerciseTemplateId": "a4e6f6ff-60cd-4cbf-a183-0b394dfd3f1f",
        "notes": "Arms overhead, legs extended, low back pressed into floor, shoulders and feet lifted. Scale it: bent knees with arms at sides (easiest) → straight legs with arms at sides → straight legs with arms overhead (hardest). If you can hold 30s at the hardest version, you can progress to hollow body rocks.",

        "sets": [

      {
        "name": "Plank",
        "index": 4,
        "exerciseTemplateId": "C6C9B8A0",
        "notes": "Side plank with top leg on bench, bottom leg free. Trains lateral core and adductors simultaneously. Regression: bend bottom knee to floor, or use a shorter bench so you're supporting from the knee instead of the foot. Progression: add hip dips (lower and lift hips while holding). This exercise alone can fix a lot of your lateral movement issues.",

content: Everything is loaded, but Hevy Planner wasn't cleared out before running setup. Also, shouldn't we use another folder for Phase 1 or whatever it's called on the Roadmap?

content.text:
Setup returned 200 in 1407ms — it completed. But "Main Sessions" and "Recovery" folders are missing. The issue is likely that `getOrCreateRoutineFolder` found the "Hevy Planner" folder for some reason, or the folder creation for those names failed silently.

Wait — I bet the problem is that `createRoutine` with `folder_id` puts the routine in the right folder, but `getOrCreateRoutineFolder` only created "Daily" and then the remaining routines fell through to the old folder. Let me check: are all the NEW routines (created at 01:43) in folder 2576657?

Yes — all 5 new non-daily routines are in folder 2576657 ("Hevy Planner"). That means `folderId` wasn't being honored. Let me check if Hevy wants `folder_id` outside the routine object.

Good points. Two issues:

1. **Old "Hevy Planner" folder wasn't cleared** — the old routines from before are still there alongside the new ones. Let me first clean up the duplicates, then we can re-examine the folder structure.

2. **Phase-based folders** — instead of generic "Main Sessions", use the actual phase name from the roadmap (e.g. "Phase 1: Foundation"). That way when Phase 2 starts, its routines go in a separate folder and Phase 1 routines stay available.

Let me check what the roadmap phases look like.

If you're doing BODi, use it for lower body days and replace upper body BODi with the skill-prep work. The false grip and straight-arm work will feel completely foreign — your tendons need time to adapt.",

file.content:
"roadmap": [
    {
      "id": "phase1",
      "name": "Phase 1: Joint Restoration",
      "weeks": "Weeks 1-8",
      "status": "current",
      "summary": "This is the program you're in now. Rebuild shoulder stability, knee resilience, Achilles health, core strength, and thoracic mobility. No skill work yet — your joints aren't ready.",
      "keyFocus": "The temptation will be to start skill work early. Don't. Every week of foundation you invest here saves you months of plateau and re-injury later. If you fail any gate test at week 8, extend Phase 1 by 2-week increments until you pass.",
      "gateTests": [
        "pain-free-planks",
        "strict-pullups-8",
        "clean-dips-15",
        "cossack-squat-full",
        "single-leg-balance-30",
        "wall-dorsiflexion-4in",
        "hollow-body-30s",
        "overhead-wall-test"
      ],
      "color": "rgb(96, 165, 224)",
      "sortOrder": 1
    },
    {
      "id": "phase2",
      "name": "Phase 2: Strength Prerequisites",
      "weeks": "Weeks 9-20 (12 weeks)",
      "status": "future",
      "summary": "Build the raw strength and positions your skills require. Shoulder strength, straight-arm conditioning, single-leg loading, and wrist preparation. Daily CARs and core work continue as maintenance.",
      "keyFocus": "This phase is dominated by pull-up volume, dip volume, and straight-arm conditioning. If you're doing BODi, use it for lower body days and replace upper body BODi with the skill-prep work. The false grip and straight-arm work will feel completely foreign — your tendons need time to adapt.",
      "gateTests": [
        "strict-pullups-12",
        "parallel-dips-15",
        "false-grip-20s",
        "ring-support-30s",
        "pistol-to-box",
        "wall-handstand-45s",
        "planche-lean-10s",
        "scapular-pullups-3x8"
      ],
      "color": "rgb(224, 134, 96)",
      "sortOrder": 2
    },
    {
      "id": "phase3",
      "name": "Phase 3: Skill Acquisition",
      "weeks": "Weeks 21-36 (16 weeks)",
      "status": "future",
      "summary": "Active skill practice begins. Muscle-up transition training, planche lean progressions, pistol squat depth development, and handstand balance work. This is as much neurological as physical.",
      "keyFocus": "Skill days replace some strength days. You're now practicing 3-4 skills per week alongside maintenance mobility and strength. The muscle-up will likely click before the planche — the transition is a technique breakthrough moment. Film every attempt.",
      "gateTests": [
        "muscle-ups-1-3",

toolUseResult.originalFile:
{
  "meta": {
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "title": "Hevy Planner",
    "subtitle": "Personalized Training Protocol",
    "description": "8-week joint restoration program with 12-month skills roadmap. Rebuilds shoulder stability, knee resilience, Achilles health, core strength, and thoracic mobility — then progresses to muscle-ups, tuck planche, pistol squats, and handstands.",
    "author": "AI Coach",
    "durationWeeks": 0,
    "tags": [
      "mobility",
      "joint-health",
      "rehabilitation",
      "calisthenics",
      "skills"
    ]
  },
  "exerciseTemplates": [
    {
      "id": "neck-cars",
      "title": "Neck CARs",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "neck",
      "notes": "Slow, controlled circles. Keep shoulders still. If you find a sticky spot, spend extra time there.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=neck+CARs+controlled+articular+rotations+tutorial",
      "searchTerms": "Neck CARs FRC tutorial"
    },
    {
      "id": "shoulder-cars",
      "title": "Shoulder CARs",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "shoulders",
      "notes": "CRITICAL for you. Make fist, lock elbow straight, trace the biggest circle possible. Go SLOW — 10 seconds per rotation minimum. Pain-free range only, expand it over weeks.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=shoulder+CARs+controlled+articular+rotations",
      "searchTerms": "Shoulder CARs FRC"
    },
    {
      "id": "hip-cars",
      "title": "Hip CARs (Standing)",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "glutes",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "hip_flexors"
      ],
      "notes": "Hold a wall for balance. Drive knee up, open out, extend back, return. Keep pelvis still — the rotation happens at the hip socket only.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=standing+hip+CARs+FRC+tutorial",
      "searchTerms": "Standing hip CARs FRC"
    },
    {
      "id": "ankle-cars",
      "title": "Ankle CARs",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "calves",
      "notes": "CRITICAL — especially right side (Achilles repair). Seated, lift foot off ground, make slow full circles. Note any clicking or restriction without forcing through it.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=ankle+CARs+controlled+articular+rotations",
      "searchTerms": "Ankle CARs FRC"
    },
    {
      "id": "spine-cars",
      "title": "Spine CARs (Segmental Cat-Cow)",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "lower_back",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "abdominals"
      ],
      "notes": "On all fours, roll through flexion and extension one vertebra at a time. Don't rush. This decompresses the spine after desk work.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=segmental+cat+cow+spine+CARs",
      "searchTerms": "Segmental cat-cow spine CARs"
    },
    {
      "id": "wrist-cars",
      "title": "Wrist CARs",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "forearms",
      "notes": "Make a fist, trace slow full circles at the wrist. You're doing planks, bear crawls, and bench pressing — your wrists take a beating. These also help you identify early warning signs of overuse before they become problems.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=wrist+CARs+controlled+articular+rotations",
      "searchTerms": "Wrist CARs FRC"
    },
    {
      "id": "tspine-rotation-quadruped",
      "title": "T-Spine Rotation (Quadruped)",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "upper_back",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "shoulders"
      ],
      "notes": "On all fours, place one hand behind your head. Rotate that elbow toward the ceiling, then down toward the opposite wrist. Follow your elbow with your eyes. This is the single most important addition for you — your desk-locked thoracic spine is the upstream bottleneck for your shoulders. If this doesn't rotate, your shoulder compensates during every press.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=quadruped+thoracic+rotation+mobility+drill",
      "searchTerms": "Quadruped thoracic rotation"
    },
    {
      "id": "dead-hangs",
      "title": "Dead Hangs",
      "type": "duration",
      "equipmentCategory": "pull_up_bar",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "lats",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "forearms",
        "shoulders"
      ],
      "notes": "Use your pull-up bar. Passive hang, let your shoulders stretch toward your ears. This decompresses the shoulder joint and builds grip. If 30s is easy, progress to active hangs (pull shoulders down while hanging).",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=dead+hang+shoulder+mobility+progression",
      "searchTerms": "Dead hang shoulder health"
    },
    {
      "id": "tspine-extension-foam-roller",
      "title": "T-Spine Extension Over Foam Roller",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "foam_roller",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "upper_back",
      "notes": "Place foam roller across your mid-back. Support your head with hands, elbows pointed forward. Extend back over the roller, then crunch forward — 10 reps. Then hold the extended position for 30 seconds. Move the roller up or down one vertebra and repeat. This directly unlocks the thoracic extension your shoulders need for overhead pressing and front rack position.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=thoracic+extension+foam+roller+mobility",
      "searchTerms": "T-spine extension foam roller",
      "tags": [
        "tspine-integration"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "open-book",
      "title": "Open Book (Thread the Needle)",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "upper_back",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "shoulders"
      ],
      "notes": "Lie on your side, knees stacked and bent at 90°. Top arm opens toward the ceiling and behind you while keeping knees together. Follow your hand with your eyes. The rotation should come from your mid-back, NOT your lower back — keeping your knees stacked prevents lumbar rotation. This is the movement pattern that lets you actually rotate during daily life without shoulder or low back compensation.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=open+book+thoracic+rotation+mobility+drill",
      "searchTerms": "Open book thoracic rotation",
      "tags": [
        "tspine-integration"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "scapular-pushups",
      "title": "Scapular Push-ups",
      "type": "bodyweight_reps",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "chest",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "shoulders"
      ],
      "notes": "Plank position, arms locked straight. Only your shoulder blades move — protract (spread apart) and retract (squeeze together). This trains serratus anterior, which is likely weak and causing your plank shoulder pain.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=scapular+push+ups+tutorial+serratus",
      "searchTerms": "Scapular push-ups serratus"
    },
    {
      "id": "banded-external-rotation",
      "title": "Banded External Rotation at 90°",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "resistance_band",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "shoulders",
      "notes": "Elbow at 90°, upper arm at shoulder height. Rotate forearm up against band resistance. Slow eccentric (3 sec lowering). This directly strengthens the rotator cuff in the position where it fails during pressing.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=banded+external+rotation+90+degrees+rotator+cuff",
      "searchTerms": "Banded external rotation 90 degrees"
    },
    {
      "id": "prone-ytw-raises",
      "title": "Prone Y-T-W Raises",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "dumbbell",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "shoulders",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "upper_back"
      ],
      "notes": "Lie face down on bench, light dumbbells (3-5 lb to start). Y = arms overhead in a V. T = arms straight out. W = elbows bent, squeeze shoulder blades. If 5 lb is too much, start bodyweight. These are humbling.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=prone+Y+T+W+raises+shoulder+rehab",
      "searchTerms": "Prone Y-T-W raises"
    },
    {
      "id": "wall-slides",
      "title": "Wall Slides",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "shoulders",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "upper_back"
      ],
      "notes": "Back and arms flat against wall. Slide arms up overhead keeping everything in contact with the wall. If your lower back arches off the wall, that's a mobility restriction — only go as high as you can maintain contact.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=wall+slides+shoulder+mobility+exercise",
      "searchTerms": "Wall slides shoulder mobility"
    },
    {
      "id": "shoulder-sleeper-stretch",
      "title": "Shoulder Sleeper Stretch with PAILs/RAILs",
      "type": "duration",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "shoulders",
      "notes": "Lie on your side, bottom arm at 90°. Use top hand to gently press into internal rotation. Hold 60s passive. Then PAILs: push bottom hand INTO the floor (10s, 20% effort building to 70%). Then RAILs: try to rotate hand UP without moving (10s same ramp). Re-stretch — you'll gain range.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=shoulder+PAILs+RAILs+sleeper+stretch+FRC",
      "searchTerms": "PAILs RAILs shoulder sleeper stretch"
    },
    {
      "id": "hollow-body-hold",
      "title": "Hollow Body Hold",
      "type": "duration",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "abdominals",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "hip_flexors"
      ],
      "notes": "Lie on your back, arms overhead, legs extended. Press lower back INTO the floor and lift shoulders and feet slightly off the ground. If your low back peels off the floor, bend your knees or bring arms to your sides to regress. This is the missing link behind your plank shoulder pain — when your core can't hold position, your shoulders compensate. Build this, and planks and pressing improve.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=hollow+body+hold+tutorial+progression",
      "searchTerms": "Hollow body hold progression",
      "tags": [
        "core-integration"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "ninety-ninety-hip-switches",
      "title": "90/90 Hip Switches",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "glutes",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "hip_flexors"
      ],
      "notes": "Sit with both legs at 90°. Rotate from one side to the other. Keep spine tall. If you can't get your knees to the floor, that's fine — work the range you have. This is your hip mobility barometer.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=90+90+hip+switches+mobility+tutorial",
      "searchTerms": "90/90 hip switches mobility"
    },
    {
      "id": "glute-bridge",
      "title": "Glute Bridge with 3-sec Hold",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "glutes",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "hamstrings"
      ],
      "notes": "Lie on back, knees bent, feet flat. Drive through heels, squeeze glutes HARD at the top, hold 3 seconds. Lower slowly. If you feel this in your hamstrings more than your glutes, bring your feet closer to your butt. This wakes up the glutes so they fire properly during squats, lunges, and walking. At 46 with desk work, your glutes are almost certainly underperforming — this fixes that.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=glute+bridge+hold+activation+tutorial",
      "searchTerms": "Glute bridge activation hold",
      "tags": [
        "glute-activation"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "banded-clamshells",
      "title": "Banded Clamshells",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "resistance_band",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "glutes",
      "notes": "Lie on your side, knees bent, band around knees. Open top knee against band resistance while keeping feet together. Slow and controlled — don't let the band snap your knee back down. This targets the gluteus medius, which stabilizes your pelvis and directly protects your knees during lateral movements. If your Cossack squats and side lunges hurt, weak glute med is likely a big contributor.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=banded+clamshell+glute+medius+activation",
      "searchTerms": "Banded clamshell glute medius",
      "tags": [
        "glute-activation"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "tibialis-raises",
      "title": "Tibialis Anterior Raises",
      "type": "bodyweight_reps",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "calves",
      "notes": "Stand with back against wall, feet ~12 inches out. Pull toes up toward shins, lower slowly. This strengthens the front of your shin and protects the knee. The Knees Over Toes Guy made this famous for good reason.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=tibialis+raises+knees+over+toes+tutorial",
      "searchTerms": "Tibialis raises Knees Over Toes"
    },
    {
      "id": "banded-tke",
      "title": "Banded Terminal Knee Extensions",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "resistance_band",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "quadriceps",
      "notes": "Attach band behind your knee to a low anchor. Stand facing away, slight bend. Squeeze to full lockout against band resistance. This isolates the VMO — the inner quad muscle that stabilizes the kneecap.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=banded+terminal+knee+extension+VMO",
      "searchTerms": "Banded TKE VMO strengthening"
    },
    {
      "id": "atg-split-squat",
      "title": "ATG Split Squat (Regressed)",
      "type": "bodyweight_reps",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "quadriceps",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "glutes",
        "calves"
      ],
      "notes": "Start with hands on bench/chair for support. Back knee tracks all the way down and forward. ONLY go as deep as you can control without pain. Weeks 1-2: use support. Weeks 3-4: reduce support. This is the single best knee bulletproofing exercise.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=ATG+split+squat+regression+beginner+knees+over+toes",
      "searchTerms": "ATG split squat beginner regression",
      "progressionByPhase": {
        "Foundation": {
          "sets": "3×8 each leg",
          "notes": "Use bench support, partial depth only."
        },
        "Build": {
          "sets": "3×8 each leg",
          "notes": "Reduce bench support, increase depth."
        },
        "Load": {
          "sets": "3×8 each leg",
          "notes": "Light dumbbells, no support."
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "cossack-squat",
      "title": "Cossack Squat (Partial ROM)",
      "type": "bodyweight_reps",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "quadriceps",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "glutes",
        "hamstrings"
      ],
      "notes": "Bodyweight only. Start with a small range — you do NOT need to go ass-to-grass. Hold a doorframe or rack for balance. This directly trains the lateral movement pattern that's giving your knees trouble. Depth will come with consistency.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=cossack+squat+beginner+progression+mobility",
      "searchTerms": "Cossack squat progression beginner",
      "progressionByPhase": {
        "Foundation": {
          "sets": "3×6 each side",
          "notes": "Hold doorframe, go only as deep as comfortable."
        },
        "Build": {
          "sets": "3×6 each side",
          "notes": "Less support, aim for parallel thigh depth."
        },
        "Load": {
          "sets": "3×6 each side",
          "notes": "Goblet Cossack Squats with light dumbbell."
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "active-frog-stretch",
      "title": "Active Frog Stretch",
      "type": "duration",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "adductors",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "hip_flexors"
      ],
      "notes": "On all fours, knees wide, rock forward and back. Keep spine neutral. This opens the adductors (inner thigh) which are contributing to both your lateral lunge pain and cross-legged sitting difficulty. Active rocking > static hold.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=active+frog+stretch+hip+adductor+mobility",
      "searchTerms": "Active frog stretch adductors"
    },
    {
      "id": "copenhagen-plank",
      "title": "Copenhagen Plank",
      "type": "duration",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "abdominals",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "adductors"
      ],
      "notes": "Side plank position with top leg on a bench, bottom leg hanging free. This is a two-for-one — it trains lateral core strength AND adductor strength simultaneously. Directly addresses your lateral lunge knee pain. Regress by using bottom knee on the floor or shortening the hold. These are brutally hard at first — 10 seconds is fine to start.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=copenhagen+plank+tutorial+regression+progression",
      "searchTerms": "Copenhagen plank tutorial progression",
      "tags": [
        "core-integration"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "extended-ankle-cars",
      "title": "Extended Ankle CARs",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "calves",
      "notes": "Slower and more deliberate than your daily CARs. Spend 5+ seconds per rotation on the right ankle. Map out the full range, note where it catches. Over weeks, the circle should get bigger.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=ankle+CARs+FRC+detailed+tutorial",
      "searchTerms": "Ankle CARs detailed FRC"
    },
    {
      "id": "eccentric-calf-raises",
      "title": "Eccentric Calf Raises (Alfredson Protocol)",
      "type": "reps_only",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "calves",
      "notes": "CRITICAL for your Achilles. Stand on step edge, raise up on BOTH feet, then lower slowly (3-4 sec) on ONE foot. Do both straight-knee (gastroc) and bent-knee (soleus) versions. The right side may feel tight — stay in a pain-free range. This is the gold standard for Achilles tendon health.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=eccentric+calf+raises+achilles+alfredson+protocol",
      "searchTerms": "Eccentric calf raises Alfredson Achilles",
      "progressionByPhase": {
        "Foundation": {
          "notes": "Both-leg lower if single-leg is too intense on the right."
        },
        "Build": {
          "notes": "Full single-leg protocol."
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "wall-dorsiflexion",
      "title": "Wall Dorsiflexion Mobilization",
      "type": "duration",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "calves",
      "notes": "Face wall, foot ~4 inches away. Drive knee forward over toes, keeping heel DOWN. Measure progress by how far from the wall you can get your foot while still touching knee to wall. Your right side will be tighter — that's the Achilles repair limiting dorsiflexion.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=wall+ankle+dorsiflexion+mobilization+test",
      "searchTerms": "Wall dorsiflexion ankle mobilization"
    },
    {
      "id": "single-leg-balance",
      "title": "Single-Leg Balance (Eyes Closed)",
      "type": "duration",
      "equipmentCategory": "none",
      "primaryMuscleGroup": "calves",
      "secondaryMuscleGroups": [
        "glutes"
      ],
      "notes": "Start with eyes open if needed. Bare feet on firm surface. The goal is rebuilding proprioception in the repaired ankle. If 30s is easy with eyes closed, try it on a folded towel for an unstable surface.",
      "videoURL": "https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=single+leg+balance+proprioception+ankle+rehab",
      "searchTerms": "Single leg balance proprioception",
      "progressionByPhase": {
        "Load": {
          "notes": "Single-leg balance on unstable surface (folded towel or pillow)."
        }
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "supported-deep-squat",
      "title": "Supported Deep Squat Hold",
      "type": "duration",

      "id": "core-stability",
      "title": "Session D: Core Stability",
      "subtitle": "15-20 min — Do 1-2x per week",
      "description": "Your shoulders, knees, and ankles all depend on a stable trunk. When the core can't hold position, your joints compensate — that's a big part of why you keep getting injured. This session trains anti-extension, anti-rotation, and anti-lateral-flexion — the three pillars of functional core stability. These aren't crunches; they teach your core to resist unwanted movement so your limbs can move freely. Exercises marked with ◆ in other sessions are drawn from here.",
      "folderGroup": "Main Sessions",
      "sortOrder": 5,
      "color": "rgb(224, 120, 150)",
      "exercises": [
        {
          "exerciseTemplateId": "dead-bugs",
          "sets": "3×10 each side",
          "notes": "The king of core exercises for your situation. Lie on back, arms up, knees at 90°. Press lower back into floor — this is the cue that matters. Extend opposite arm and leg while maintaining ZERO low back movement. Slow and controlled — 3 seconds out, 3 seconds back. If your back arches, shorten the range. Progress by holding a light dumbbell overhead or adding a band around your feet."
        },
        {
          "exerciseTemplateId": "pallof-press",
          "sets": "3×10 each side"
        },
        {
          "exerciseTemplateId": "bird-dogs",
          "sets": "3×8 each side"
        },
        {
          "exerciseTemplateId": "hollow-body-hold",
          "sets": "3×30 sec",
          "notes": "Arms overhead, legs extended, low back pressed into floor, shoulders and feet lifted. Scale it: bent knees with arms at sides (easiest) → straight legs with arms at sides → straight legs with arms overhead (hardest). If you can hold 30s at the hardest version, you can progress to hollow body rocks."
        },
        {
          "exerciseTemplateId": "copenhagen-plank",
          "sets": "3×20-30 sec each side",
          "notes": "Side plank with top leg on bench, bottom leg free. Trains lateral core and adductors simultaneously. Regression: bend bottom knee to floor, or use a shorter bench so you're supporting from the knee instead of the foot. Progression: add hip dips (lower and lift hips while holding). This exercise alone can fix a lot of your lateral movement issues."
        },
        {
          "exerciseTemplateId": "suitcase-carry",
          "sets": "3×30 sec each side"
        }
      ]

        "Eccentric calf raises: both-leg lower if single-leg is too intense on the right",
        "Dead hangs: aim for 15-20s, build to 30s",
        "Core: Dead bugs with bent knees, hollow body with knees bent and arms at sides, Copenhagen plank from knees (10-15s)"
      ]

      "color": "rgb(224, 134, 96)",
      "currentState": "3-5 pull-ups (previously 7-10 before shoulder issues). No muscle-up experience. Shoulder pain during planks and pressing.",
      "requirements": "12+ strict pull-ups, 15+ dips, false grip strength, explosive pulling power, deep shoulder extension under load, and the transition technique.",
      "gapAnalysis": "Your pulling strength base exists but is suppressed by shoulder instability. Once shoulders stabilize (Phase 1), pull-up numbers will rebound quickly since the muscle memory is there. The false grip and transition are completely new skills. The deep shoulder extension required at the transition point is the riskiest part given your shoulder history — this is why we must not rush.",
      "timeline": "First muscle-up: 6-9 months (weeks 24-36). Confident reps: 9-12 months.",

The good news: tuck planche is a realistic 9-15 month goal, well within the same timeline as your muscle-up. Advanced tuck (legs partially extended) is the stretch goal beyond that.",
      "timeline": "Planche lean: 4-6 months. Frog stand: 3-4 months. Tuck planche (2-3 sec): 9-12 months. Tuck planche (5-10 sec): 12-15 months. Advanced tuck planche: 15-24 months (stretch goal).",

  "benchmarks": [
    {
      "id": "wall-dorsiflexion",
      "name": "Wall Dorsiflexion Test",
      "howTo": "Face wall, foot back from wall. Drive knee forward keeping heel down. Measure max distance (inches) where knee touches wall and heel stays down.",
      "target": "Left: 4-5 inches. Right (Achilles): will start lower — track improvement, don't compare sides.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 2 weeks",
      "unit": "inches"
    },
    {
      "id": "single-leg-balance-test",
      "name": "Single-Leg Balance (Eyes Closed)",
      "howTo": "Stand on one foot, close eyes, time how long before you touch down or open eyes.",
      "target": "30 seconds each side. Right ankle will lag — that's expected.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 2 weeks",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "hollow-body-hold-test",
      "name": "Hollow Body Hold Time",
      "howTo": "Full position (arms overhead, legs straight). Time until low back lifts off floor.",
      "target": "Week 1: 10-15s is normal. Week 8 goal: 30-45s.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 3 weeks",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "overhead-reach-test",
      "name": "Overhead Reach (Wall Test)",
      "howTo": "Stand with back, butt, and head against wall. Raise arms overhead trying to touch thumbs to wall without arching your low back off the wall.",
      "target": "Thumbs touch wall with back flat = good t-spine and shoulder mobility. If back arches, that's your t-spine restriction.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 3 weeks"
    },
    {
      "id": "deep-squat-test",
      "name": "Deep Squat Assessment",
      "howTo": "Feet shoulder width, squat as deep as possible without any support. Note: heels lifting, excessive forward lean, knees caving, inability to keep chest up.",
      "target": "Hips below parallel, heels down, chest up, no pain. Use a phone to film yourself — you'll miss compensations otherwise.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, film it. Re-film every 4 weeks."
    },
    {
      "id": "ninety-ninety-hip-test",
      "name": "90/90 Hip Test",
      "howTo": "Sit in 90/90 position (both legs at 90°). Can you sit tall with both knees touching or near the floor?",
      "target": "Both knees within 3-4 inches of floor, spine upright without hands for support.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 3 weeks"
    },
    {
      "id": "cross-body-shoulder-reach",
      "name": "Cross-Body Shoulder Reach",
      "howTo": "Reach right hand behind your back from below, left hand from above. How close can your fingertips get? Repeat on the other side.",
      "target": "Fingers touching or overlapping. Note the difference between sides — your tighter side is the priority.",
      "frequency": "Test day 1, then every 4 weeks"
    },
    {
      "id": "pain-free-planks",
      "name": "Pain-Free Planks",
      "howTo": "Hold a standard plank position. Note any shoulder pain during or after.",
      "target": "45+ seconds with zero shoulder pain."
    },
    {
      "id": "strict-pullups-8",
      "name": "Strict Pull-ups (Phase 1 Gate)",
      "howTo": "Dead hang start, pull chin over bar, full lockout between reps. No kipping.",
      "target": "8-10 strict pull-ups."
    },
    {
      "id": "clean-dips-15",
      "name": "Clean Dips (Phase 1 Gate)",
      "howTo": "Parallel bars or bench dips, full range of motion, controlled tempo.",
      "target": "15+ clean dips."
    },
    {
      "id": "cossack-squat-full",
      "name": "Cossack Squat Full Depth",
      "howTo": "Full-depth bodyweight Cossack squat each side, no external support.",
      "target": "Full depth both sides, no support, no pain."
    },
    {
      "id": "single-leg-balance-30",
      "name": "Single-Leg Balance 30s (Gate)",
      "howTo": "Stand on one foot, close eyes. Time until loss of balance.",
      "target": "30 seconds each side, eyes closed.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "wall-dorsiflexion-4in",
      "name": "Wall Dorsiflexion 4+ Inches",
      "howTo": "Face wall, drive knee forward. Measure distance from wall where heel stays down.",
      "target": "4+ inches each side.",
      "unit": "inches"
    },
    {
      "id": "hollow-body-30s",
      "name": "Hollow Body Hold 30s (Gate)",
      "howTo": "Arms overhead, legs straight. Hold until low back lifts off floor.",
      "target": "30+ seconds, arms overhead.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "overhead-wall-test",
      "name": "Overhead Wall Test (Gate)",
      "howTo": "Back against wall, raise arms overhead keeping back flat.",
      "target": "Thumbs touch wall, back stays flat."
    },
    {
      "id": "strict-pullups-12",
      "name": "Strict Pull-ups (Phase 2 Gate)",
      "howTo": "Dead hang start, strict form, full range.",
      "target": "12-15 strict pull-ups."
    },
    {
      "id": "parallel-dips-15",
      "name": "Parallel Bar Dips (Phase 2 Gate)",
      "howTo": "Parallel bars, full depth, controlled tempo.",
      "target": "15-20 parallel bar dips."
    },
    {
      "id": "false-grip-20s",
      "name": "False Grip Hang",
