      "target": "20+ seconds.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "ring-support-30s",
      "name": "Ring Support Hold",
      "howTo": "Support position on rings, shoulders externally rotated (rings turned out).",
      "target": "30-second hold with external rotation.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "pistol-to-box",
      "name": "Pistol Squat to Box",
      "howTo": "Stand on one leg, lower to bench/box (12-16 inch height), stand back up.",
      "target": "Controlled descent and ascent each leg."
    },
    {
      "id": "wall-handstand-45s",
      "name": "Wall Handstand Hold",
      "howTo": "Stomach facing wall, nose almost touching. Hold inverted position.",
      "target": "45-second hold.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "planche-lean-10s",
      "name": "Planche Lean",
      "howTo": "Hands on floor, lean forward shifting weight over hands at 20+ degree angle.",
      "target": "10-second hold at 20+ degrees.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "scapular-pullups-3x8",
      "name": "Scapular Pull-ups",
      "howTo": "From dead hang, depress shoulders (pull shoulder blades down), return to passive hang.",
      "target": "3×8 full range on bar."
    },
    {
      "id": "muscle-ups-1-3",
      "name": "Muscle-ups (Phase 3 Gate)",
      "howTo": "From dead hang, pull up and transition over the bar or rings to support position.",
      "target": "1-3 muscle-ups (bar or rings)."
    },
    {
      "id": "tuck-planche-5s",
      "name": "Tuck Planche Hold",
      "howTo": "Hands on floor or parallettes, tuck knees to chest, lift hips level with shoulders.",
      "target": "5-second hold on floor or parallettes."
    },
    {
      "id": "pistol-squat-full",
      "name": "Full Pistol Squat",
      "howTo": "Single-leg squat to full depth. Counterbalance with a light weight is acceptable.",
      "target": "Full-depth each leg, controlled tempo."
    },
    {
      "id": "freestanding-handstand-20s",
      "name": "Freestanding Handstand",
      "howTo": "Kick up to handstand in open space. Hold without wall support.",
      "target": "20-second hold OR 45-second wall handstand with controlled entry/exit.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "negative-muscle-up-3s",
      "name": "Negative Muscle-up",
      "howTo": "Start in support position on bar, lower slowly through transition to hang.",
      "target": "3-second controlled descent.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "tuck-planche-level",
      "name": "Tuck Planche (Hips Level)",
      "howTo": "Tuck planche with hips at shoulder height, back flat.",
      "target": "Hips level with shoulders, held position."
    },
    {
      "id": "consecutive-muscle-ups-3-5",
      "name": "Consecutive Muscle-ups",
      "howTo": "Multiple muscle-ups in a row without dismounting.",
      "target": "3-5 consecutive muscle-ups."
    },
    {
      "id": "tuck-planche-10s",
      "name": "Tuck Planche 10s Hold",
      "howTo": "Tuck planche position held for duration.",
      "target": "10+ seconds.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "freestanding-handstand-15s",
      "name": "Freestanding Handstand 15s",
      "howTo": "Freestanding handstand held for duration.",
      "target": "15+ seconds.",
      "unit": "seconds"
    },
    {
      "id": "pistol-squat-no-counterbalance",
      "name": "Full Pistol Squat (No Counterbalance)",
      "howTo": "Full-depth single-leg squat, arms free (no weight held for balance).",
      "target": "Full pistol each leg, no counterbalance."
    },
    {
      "id": "advanced-tuck-planche-3-5s",

      "target": "3-5 seconds (stretch goal).",

  "foundations": [
    {
      "id": "breathing-bracing",
      "title": "Breathing & Bracing Protocol",
      "description": "Proper breathing and bracing is the foundation of all loaded movement. Master this before adding intensity to any exercise.",
      "steps": [
        {
          "step": 1,
          "name": "Find Your Diaphragm",
          "instructions": "Lie on your back, knees bent. Place one hand on your chest, one on your belly. Breathe in through your nose — ONLY the belly hand should rise. Practice until you can take 10 breaths where only your belly moves.",
          "videoSearch": "diaphragmatic breathing tutorial lying"
        },
        {
          "step": 2,
          "name": "360 Breathing",
          "instructions": "Place hands on your sides. When you inhale, your belly, sides, AND lower back should all expand outward like inflating a cylinder. Practice 10 breaths in the 90/90 position.",
          "videoSearch": "360 degree breathing core stability drill"
        },
        {
          "step": 3,
          "name": "Bracing Under Load",
          "instructions": "Take a 360 breath in. Without exhaling, tighten your midsection as if someone is about to punch you in the gut. Inhale at the easy part, brace, execute the hard part, exhale at the top.",
          "videoSearch": "bracing core squat university breathing"
        }
      ],
      "practice": "Do 10 diaphragmatic breaths before daily CARs for the first 2 weeks.",
      "activeDuringWeeks": {
        "start": 1,
        "end": 4
      }
    }
  ],

lines: "description": "Your shoulders are compensating for weak scapular stabilizers and rotator cuff. This session rebuilds the foundation so your pressing strength can actually express itself without pain. Within 3-4 weeks, planks and overhead work should start feeling noticeably better.",

"description": "Your lateral lunge pain and cross-legged sitting difficulty both point to tight hip adductors and weak VMO (inner quad). This session opens the hips and builds the knee stability that lets you load lateral movements safely. The Cossack squat is your eventual goal movement.",

"description": "Your repaired Achilles needs consistent eccentric loading to stay resilient — this is backed by decades of research (Alfredson protocol). Combined with dorsiflexion work, this session protects the ankle and unlocks deeper squat positions. The balance work re-trains proprioception that was disrupted by the surgery.",

"description": "Your shoulders, knees, and ankles all depend on a stable trunk. When the core can't hold position, your joints compensate — that's a big part of why you keep getting injured. This session trains anti-extension, anti-rotation, and anti-lateral-flexion — the three pillars of functional core stability. These aren't crunches; they teach your core to resist unwanted movement so your limbs can move freely. Exercises marked with ◆ in other sessions are drawn from here.",

content.text:
Now let me first clean up Hevy — delete all the old duplicates and the "Hevy Planner" folder, then re-run setup. Let me use the cleanup endpoint, but first I need to delete the old routines that are in "Hevy Planner" folder manually since they're not duplicates (same title, different folder).

Actually, let me just delete all routines and folders, then do a completely clean setup. The easiest approach since this is a reset.

input.command: cat /Users/ryanr/.claude/projects/-Users-ryanr-Code-hevy-planner/efca647b-adeb-4816-9a5e-8d564bffcace/tool-results/toolu_01MsagbpvzeystnnBbtvEhZV.json | node -e "const d=JSON.parse(require('fs').readFileSync('/dev/stdin','utf8')); const routines=JSON.parse(d[0].text); routines.forEach(r => console.log(r.id))"
input.description: List all routine IDs for deletion

The updated JSON is at `programs/mobility-joint-restoration.json` with the new values. You'll need to re-upload it. But this raises a question: should the setup page offer the bundled program as a default so you don't have to re-download each time?

For now — delete the Hevy folders again, I'll reset the DB, and you can upload the updated JSON. Or I can check how the setup form works and see if we can streamline this.

content.text:
Found it. The setup form requires the user to upload a JSON file — there's no "use the bundled default" option. The "Download the starter program" link at line 38 points to `/programs/default.json` for download.

The program JSON always comes from the user's upload. So even though the bundled JSON has `folderGroup`, the user has to download and re-upload it.

Let me check if there's a route serving `/programs/default.json`.

The default program is already bundled and served at `/programs/default.json`. The simplest fix: when no file is uploaded, use the bundled default automatically instead of requiring an upload. Let me update the setup to pre-populate with the default.

All passing. That was the root cause of all the duplication — `folderId` vs `folder_id`. The reconciliation was comparing against `undefined` every time, so it never matched existing routines.

The dev server auto-reloaded. The existing routines in Hevy now have the right folders. If you reset DB and re-run setup, it should match them by title+folder and update in place instead of duplicating. Want to test that, or clean up the duplicates from this run first?

message.content: Daily is back. Deep linking works, at least on the web app. Will this open in the mobile app on my phone?

content.text:
The deep link (`hevy.com/routine/{id}`) opens a web page that has a Branch.io redirect prompting to open in the Hevy app. So on your phone it should:

1. Open the Hevy web page briefly
2. Prompt "Open in Hevy?" (or auto-redirect if you have universal links enabled)
3. Open the routine directly in the Hevy app

It works best if you install the app as a PWA on your home screen — then the link tap behavior is smoother. Try it on your phone and let me know if it actually opens the routine in the Hevy app or gets stuck on the web page.

Want me to commit all these changes now?

message.content: commit, push, and then let's deploy to Cloudflare, assuming Cloudflare Access is set up
